evals.name: Design a crash recovery benchmark
evals.prompt: I built a multi-agent coordination system where 12 agents collaborate on code tasks. When an agent crashes, the system redistributes its work using session context. I want to prove this is better than naive reassignment. Design the experiment for me. I can run about 200 trials total.
expected_behaviors: Identifies at least two baselines: one Tier A (context-aware round-robin or similar strong alternative) and one Tier B (naive/random), not just 'no coordination'
Calculates sample size allocation across conditions given the 200-trial budget (roughly 65-70 per condition for 3 conditions) and notes this is marginal for medium effect sizes
Specifies both automated metrics (salvage latency, completion rate) and human-evaluated metrics (recovery fidelity) with concrete rubrics
Includes inter-rater reliability requirements (Cohen's kappa >= 0.60) for human evaluation
Recommends non-parametric tests for latency (Kruskal-Wallis) with justification
States Bonferroni or Holm correction for multiple primary metrics
Includes a threats-to-validity section addressing scale (12 agents != 100 agents) and crash model limitations
anti_behaviors: Suggests comparing only to 'no coordination at all' (strawman baseline, Tier F)
Reports only means without requiring confidence intervals or variance measures
Recommends parametric tests for latency data without checking normality
Ignores multiple comparison correction when testing multiple metrics
Omits human evaluation entirely for quality/fidelity judgments

evals.name: Audit suspicious benchmark results
evals.prompt: Here are results from our coordination protocol benchmark: We tested 4 systems across 15 metrics. System D (ours) was statistically significant (p < 0.05) on 6 of 15 metrics. All tests were t-tests. We ran 20 trials per system. Mean latencies: System A 450ms, System B 380ms, System C 520ms, System D 290ms. No confidence intervals or effect sizes reported. What's wrong with this analysis?
expected_behaviors: Identifies multiple comparison problem: 15 metrics x 6 pairwise comparisons = 90 tests, expecting ~4.5 false positives at alpha=0.05, so 6 'significant' results is barely above chance
Flags missing confidence intervals and effect sizes as a critical omission (failure mode 12c)
Questions whether t-tests are appropriate for latency data (likely skewed, should check normality or use non-parametric)
Notes 20 trials per system is underpowered for medium effect sizes (need ~64 per group)
Identifies potential p-hacking: were these 6 metrics pre-registered or found post-hoc?
Recommends Bonferroni or Holm correction, recalculating which results survive
Requests standard deviations or IQRs to assess practical significance
anti_behaviors: Accepts the 6 significant results at face value without questioning multiple comparisons
Does not question the choice of parametric t-tests for latency
Focuses only on one issue instead of identifying the multiple overlapping problems

evals.name: Design human evaluation for salvage fidelity
evals.prompt: We need human raters to judge whether our salvage system recovers agent work faithfully. We have 50 crash-recovery scenarios and outputs from 2 systems. We can recruit 4 raters who are experienced engineers. Design the complete human evaluation protocol, including the rubric, the reliability check, and what to do if raters disagree a lot.
expected_behaviors: Designs a concrete anchored rating scale (not vague 'good/bad') with at least 4-5 levels and behavioral anchors for each level
Specifies blinding: raters must not know which system produced which output
Requires a calibration/pilot phase (e.g., 5-10 practice scenarios with discussion before real rating)
Specifies Cohen's kappa (or Fleiss' kappa for 4 raters) with the threshold: kappa < 0.40 means stop and revise rubric
Provides a disagreement resolution protocol (e.g., majority vote for 4 raters, or discussion-to-consensus)
Recommends randomizing presentation order of outputs across raters
Addresses rater fatigue: 50 scenarios x 2 outputs = 100 evaluations per rater, suggests session breaks
Specifies how to aggregate ratings for statistical analysis (median of raters, or use all ratings with rater as random effect)
anti_behaviors: Provides only a vague rubric like '1=poor, 5=excellent' without behavioral anchors
Does not mention blinding the raters to condition
Suggests averaging all rater scores without checking agreement first
